elements.sent-2411-001: Obtaining explicit stability estimates in classical functional inequalities like the Sobolev inequality has been an essentially open question for 30 years, after the celebrated but non-constructive result~\cite{MR1124290 of G.~Bianchi and H.~Egnell in 1991.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently, new methods have emerged which provide some clues on these fascinating questions.
elements.sent-2411-003: The goal of the course is to give an introduction to the topic for some fundamental functional inequalities and present several methods that can be used to obtain explicit estimates.
elements.sent-2411-005: Equality in~\eqref{Sobolev holds if and only if $f$ is in the $(d+2)$-dimensional manifold $\mathscr M$ of the Aubin--Talenti functions \begin{equation}g_{a,b,c}(x)=c\(a+|x-b|^2\)^{-\frac{d-2}2}\,,\quad a\in(0,\infty)\,,\quad b\in\R^d\,,\quad c\in\R\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-006: We refer to~\cite{frank2023sharp for an up-to-date introduction to Ineq.~\eqref{Sobolev .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: The method of~\cite{MR1124290 uses compactness and provides no estimate on $\kappa_d$.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Recently, several constructive results have been obtained, i.e., results with explicit estimates of $\kappa_d$.
elements.sent-2411-010: The goal of these lecture notes is to make a short review based on two main strategies:.
elements.sent-2411-011: The strategy of G.~Bianchi and H.~Egnell made explicit in both of its steps: the global-to-local reduction and the local analysis, both relying on methods of the Calculus of Variations,.
elements.sent-2411-012: Entropy methods and fast diffusion equations, in which we can recognize also the same two steps, that are based on nonlinear parabolic flows and carr\'e du champ techniques.
elements.sent-2411-013: Entropy methods usually result in better estimates of the stability constant, but to the price of some limitations.
elements.sent-2411-014: We will also state some results for related inequalities, like some families of Gagliardo-Nirenberg interpolation inequalities and logarithmic Sobolev inequalities, on the Euclidean space $\R^d$ or on the unit sphere $\S^d\subset\R^{d+1}$, and consider the use of the Legendre duality applied, for instance, to Hardy-Littlewood-Sobolev inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-015: Let us start with a first example of fast diffusion flows applied to inequalities in the context of the Legendre duality.
elements.sent-2411-016: On $\R^d$ with $d\ge3$, the Hardy-Littlewood-Sobolev inequality \be{HLS \nrm g{\frac{2\,d {d+2 ^2\ge\mathsf S_d\ird{g\,(-\Delta)^{-1 g}\quad\forall\,g\in\mathrm L^\frac{2\,d {d+2 (\R^d) \ee is obtained by Legendre's duality from~\eqref{Sobolev , as observed in~\cite{Lieb-83 .}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-039: Notice that stability results based on duality are known for (logarithmic) Hardy-Littlewood-Sobolev inequalities from~\cite{MR3640893,carlen2024stabilitylogarithmichardylittlewoodsobolevinequality .}
elements.sent-2411-040: The method based on~\eqref{Eqn:Yamabe also applies to the fractional Sobolev inequality and the dual Hardy-Littlewood-Sobolev inequality as shown in~\cite{jankowiak2014fractionalsobolevhardylittlewoodsobolevinequalities .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Even if the optimal value of the stability constant $\mathscr C$ is not known, a nice feature of Theorem~\ref{Theorem:ExplicitGap0 is that we have a rather precise estimate of $\mathscr C$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, a significant drawback of the method is that the deficit of the Hardy-Littlewood-Sobolev inequality corresponds to a weaker notion of distance to $\mathscr M$ than what can be expected.
elements.section-2411-002: Stability, fast diffusion equation and entropy methods
elements.sent-2411-043: On $\R^d$, let us consider the fast diffusion equation written in self-similar variables, \be{RFD \frac{\partial v {\partial t +\nabla\cdot\Big(v\,\big(\nabla v^{m-1 -\,2\,x\big)\Big)=0\,.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: By fast diffusion, we mean $m<1$ and reserve the expression porous medium to the regime $m>1$ while the heat equation case corresponds to $m=1$ (in this case, Eq~\eqref{RFD is known as the Fokker-Planck equation, and $v^{m-1}$ should be replaced by $-\,\log v$).}
elements.sent-2411-045: An introduction can be found in the two classical books~\cite{MR2176734,MR2282669 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Here we are interested in the range $m_c:=(d-2)/d<m<1$ in which nonnegative solution with initial data in $\mathrm L^1(\R^d)$ at $t=0$ exist for any $t\ge0$ according to~\cite{Herrero1985 , and more specifically to the range $m_1\le m<1$ where $m_1:=(d-1)/d$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In any case,~\eqref{RFD admits a stationary solution \begin{equation}\mathcal B(x):=\(1+|x|^2\)^\frac1{m-1}\end{equation} which is usually called a Barenblatt solution and has finite mass $M>0$ for any $m\in(m_c,1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: If $m\in(m_c,1)$, the mass of any nonnegative solution $v$ is conserved and for simplicity, we shall assume that $\ird{v(t,x) =M$ for any $t\ge0$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: See~\cite{vazquez2023surveymassconservationrelated for an interesting survey on mass conservation and the role of Barenblatt solutions in the range $(m_c,1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: With this notation, we can introduce the generalized entropy (or free energy) and Fisher information functionals respectively given~by \begin{equation}\mathcal F[v]:=\frac1{m-1}\ird{\big(v^m-\mathcal B^m-m\,\mathcal B^{m-1}\,(v-\mathcal B)\big)}\,, \mathcal I[v]:=\frac m{1-m}\ird{v\left|\nabla v^{m-1}-\nabla\mathcal B^{m-1}\right|^2}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\mathcal F[v]:=\frac1{m-1}\ird{\big(v^m-\mathcal B^m-m\,\mathcal B^{m-1}\,(v-\mathcal B)\big)}\,, \mathcal I[v]:=\frac m{1-m}\ird{v\left|\nabla v^{m-1}-\nabla\mathcal B^{m-1}\right|^2}\,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-053: A straightforward computation shows that, for a solution of~\eqref{RFD , we have \be{ddtF \frac d{dt}\mathcal F[v(t,\cdot)]=-\,\mathcal I[v(t,\cdot)]\,.. \ee.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: If $v$ is a nonnegative solution of~\eqref{RFD with initial datum $v_0\in\mathrm L^1(\R^d)$ of mass $M$ such that $\mathcal F[v_0]$ is finite, then \be{FDE:rate \mathcal F[v(t,\cdot)]\le\mathcal F[v_0]\,e^{-\,4\,t}\quad\forall\,t\ge0\,.. \ee.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The equivalence is obtained using \begin{equation}p=\frac 1{2\,m-1}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad m=\frac{p+1}{2\,p}\end{equation} with $v=f^{2\,p}$ so that $v^m=f^{p+1}$ and $v\,|\nabla v^{m-1 |^2=(p-1)^2\,|\nabla f|^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The range $m\in(m_1,1)$ means that $2\,p\in(2,2^*)$ where $2^*=2\,d/(d-2)$ if $d\ge3$ and $2^*=+\infty$ otherwise.

elements.sent-2411-078: We have obtained an improved entropy~-- entropy production inequality and an improved decay rate of $\mathcal F[v(t,\cdot)]$ by comparing the nonlinear free energy $\mathcal F$ and the Fisher information $\mathcal I$ with their linearized counterparts $\mathsf F$ and $\mathsf I$, and by getting rid of the eigenspace corresponding to $\alpha=1$ in~\eqref{Ineq:Hardy-Poincare .}

See~\cite{BDNS2021 for details.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Under Condition~\eqref{A , we have an improved decay rate for any $t$ in the asymptotic time range $[t_\star,+\infty)$.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The next question is to find an improvement in the initial time layer $[0,t_\star)$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-088: A key ingredient is the carr\'e du champ method for which we can refer to~\cite[Chapter~2]{BDNS2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-089: We learn for instance that for some strictly convex function~$\psi$ with $\psi(0)=0$, $\psi'(0)=0$, there is an improved entropy~-- entropy production inequality \begin{equation}\mathcal I-4\,\mathcal F\ge\psi(\mathcal F)\ge0\,,\end{equation} so that better decay rates can be expected if $\mathcal F$ is large, which is precisely what can be expected in the initial time layer.
elements.sent-2411-090: In fact, a key estimate of the carr\'e du champ method is the simpler observation that \begin{equation}\frac d{dt}\mathcal I[v(t,\cdot)]\le-\,4\,\mathcal I[v(t,\cdot)]\end{equation} if $v$ solves~\eqref{RFD , which allows us to prove a backward in time estimate.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Let us define $\mathcal Q[v]:=\frac{\mathcal I[v] {\mathcal F[v]}$ and notice that, using~\eqref{ddtF , we have \begin{equation}\frac d{dt}\mathcal Q[v(t,\cdot)]\le\mathcal Q[v(t,\cdot)]\,\big(\mathcal Q[v(t,\cdot)]-4\big)\,.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: If for some $\eta>0$ and $t_\star\ge0$, we have $\mathcal Q[v(t_\star,\cdot)]\ge4+\eta$, then \begin{equation}\mathcal Q[v(t,\cdot)]\ge\,4+\zeta\quad\forall\,t\in[0,t_\star]\quadwith\quad\zeta:=\frac{4\,\eta\,e^{-4\,t_\star}}{4+\eta-\eta\,e^{-4\,t_\star}}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-093: In the subcritical range with $m>m_1$, the first consequence of Lemma~\ref{prop.backward is an improved decay rate of the free energy for the solution of~\eqref{RFD .}}

elements.sent-2411-096: The second consequence of Lemma~\ref{prop.backward is a stability result for the entropy -- entropy production inequality, which reads as \begin{equation}\mathcal I[v]-\,4\,\mathcal F[v]\ge\frac\zeta{4+\zeta}\,\mathcal I[v]\end{equation} where the left-hand side is the deficit in~\eqref{EEP , while the distance in the right-hand side is measured by the Fisher information, which is a quantity that controls strong norms.}}

elements.sent-2411-100: Extending the subcritical result of Corollary~\ref{Cor:Improvedrate to the critical case uses the introduction of a time delay, as shown in~\cite[Chapter~6]{BDNS2021 .}}

elements.sent-2411-102: A remarkable feature in Theorem~\ref{Thm:Main is that we measure the distance to a specific Aubin--Talenti function and not to the manifold $\mathscr M$.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Summing up, we have obtained a construction of an explicit stability result using the fast diffusion equation and entropy methods based on a parabolic regularity theory made explicit, spectral estimates and various features of the carr\'e du champ method.
elements.sent-2411-104: However, estimates of the stability constants are way smaller than the expected values of the optimal stability constants and are available only under the restriction~\eqref{A on the tails of the functions, which is inherent to the method, as one can prove that $t_\star$ diverges if $A\to+\infty$ in~\eqref{A .}}
elements.section-2411-003: Explicit stability results for Sobolev and log-Sobolev inequalities, with optimal dimensional dependence
elements.sent-2411-105: In this section, we just state the main results and refer the reader to~\cite{https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2209.08651 for detailed results and to~\cite{DEFFL2024 for a shorter introduction to the main tools.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The most important fact is that the method gives an explicit value to the constant in the stability inequality~\eqref{stab , but again one can suspect that the lower bound is not very good, even if it has an optimal dimensional dependence.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Here we take on $\S^d$ the uniform probability measure.
elements.sent-2411-109: Let $v\in\mathrm H^1(\mathbb R^n,dx)$ with compact support, $d\ge n$ large enough and consider \begin{equation}u_d(\omega)=v\Big(\tfrac1{r_d}\,\omega_1,\tfrac1{r_d}\,\omega_2,\ldots,\tfrac1{r_d}\,\omega_n\Big)\,,\quad r_d=\sqrt{\tfrac d{2\,\pi}}\end{equation} where $\omega=(\omega_1,\omega_2,\ldots,\omega_{d+1 )\in\S^d\subset\R^{d+1}$.}

elements.section-2411-004: More explicit stability results for the logarithmic Sobolev and Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequalities on $\S^d$
elements.sent-2411-130: Here the goal is to reconciliate the flow methods of Section 2 with stability estimates without restrictions as in Section 3 and get estimate which may have the right order of magnitude compared to the optimal stability constants.
elements.sent-2411-132: If $d\ge3$, the limit case $p=2^*$ of~\eqref{GNS:Sd is the Sobolev inequality on the sphere.}
elements.sent-2411-133: As another limit case when $p\to2$, we obtain the logarithmic Sobolev inequality on the sphere \be{LSI:Sd \isd{|\nabla F|^2}\ge d\,\mathcal E_2[F]:=\frac d2\isd{F^2\,\log\(\frac{F^2 {\nrms F2^2}\)}\quad\forall\,F\in\mathrm H^1(\S^d,d\mu) \ee where $d\mu$ is the uniform probability on $\S^d$.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: While equality is always achieved by constant functions, the optimal constant $d$ can be identified with the first positive eigenvalue of the Laplace-Beltrami operator on $\S^d$.
elements.sent-2411-136: Let $X_1$ be the corresponding eigenspace of spherical harmonic functions and denote by $\mathrm \Pi_1$ the orthogonal projection onto $X_1$.
elements.sent-2411-137: If $\varphi\in X_1$ and $F_\varepsilon=1+\varepsilon\,\varphi$, a simple Taylor expansion shows that $\nrms{\nabla F_\varepsilon 2^2-d\,\mathcal E_p[F_\varepsilon]=O(\varepsilon^4)$, so that there is no hope to control the deficit from below by the square of a distance based on usual norm, at least in the direction of $X_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-139: This results is a consequence of the stability analysis of~\cite{Frank_2022 , of explicit improvements of~\eqref{GNS:Sd and~\eqref{LSI:Sd obtained for instance in~\cite{DEKL2014,1504 based on~\cite{MR2381156,MR3229793 , which involve a generalized carr\'e du champ method for the flow \begin{equation}\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}=u^{-p\,(1-m)}\(\Delta u+(m\,p-1)\,\tfrac{|\nabla u|^2}u\)\end{equation} with exponents $m\in\big[m_-(d,p),m_+(d,p)\big]$ where \begin{equation}m_\pm(d,p):=\frac1{(d+2)\,p}\(d\,p+2\pm\sqrt{d\,(p-1)\,\big(2\,d-(d-2)\,p\big)}\)\,,\end{equation} and of spectral properties taken from the proof of~\cite{MR1230930 .}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-142: In Theorem~\ref{Thm:sphere-GNS-stability we have the satisfaction to obtain a stability result which distinguishes the $X_1$ eigenspace, is based on a fast diffusion equation and does not involve any restriction on the function space, but the estimate is not uniform as $p\to2^*$, as could be expected from Theorem~\ref{DEFFL .}}

elements.sent-2411-144: In Corollary~\ref{logsob , the stability of the Gaussian logarithmic Sobolev inequality is measured only by a $\mathrm L^2(\R^n,d\gamma)$ norm.}

elements.sent-2411-146: The quest for optimal conditions is open but as an illustration, we can conclude with a last result.
elements.sent-2411-147: For any $\varepsilon>0$, there is some explicit $\mathscr C>1$ depending only on $\varepsilon$ such that, for any $u\in\mathrm H^1(\R^d,d\gamma)$ with \begin{equation}\irdg{\left(1,x\right)\,|u|^2}=(1,0)\,,\quad\irdg{|u|^2\,e^{\,\varepsilon\,|x|^2}}<\infty\end{equation} for some $\varepsilon>0$, then we have \begin{equation}\nrmG{\nabla u}2^2\ge\frac{\mathscr C}2\irdg{|u|^2\,\log|u|^2}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-148: Moreover, if $u$ is compactly supported in a ball of radius $R>0$, we know from~\cite{lee2003geometrical that with $\mathscr C=1+\frac{\mathscr C_\star(\mathsf K_\star)-1 {1+R^2\,\mathscr C_\star(\mathsf K_\star)}$, $\mathscr C_\star(\mathsf K)=1+\frac1{432\,\mathsf K}$ and $\mathsf K_\star:=\,\max\big(d,\frac{(d+1)\,R^2 {1+R^2}\big)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Here we use the heat flow case corresponding to the limit $m=1$ of the fast diffusion flow.

elements.sent-2411-152: One can certainly object that $1/432\approx0.00231481$ does not provide us with a glorious improvement of the constant, but this is definitely a step in the understanding of explicit stability results and, as a consequence, better estimates of decay rates in nonlinear evolution problems.
